title: The Version of You That Kept Going
summary: Some versions of you deserve compassion for surviving what they did not yet understand.
excerpt: Some versions of you deserve compassion for surviving what they did not yet understand.
paragraphs: There are versions of yourself you may not know how to feel proud of.

Not because they were evil or careless or beyond grace. But because when you look back, you see the choices you would not make now. The words you wish you had swallowed. The silence you wish you had broken. The doors you stayed outside of for too long, or the ones you entered before you were ready.
Looking back can be cruel if you only do it with the knowledge you have now.

You forget what you did not know then. You forget how limited your language was. You forget the fear, the confusion, the pressure, the loneliness, the need to survive the day in front of you. You judge an older version of yourself with tools that version did not yet have.
I have been thinking about that.

About the version of you that kept going before the lessons had names. The version that carried more than people understood and still found a way to answer messages, attend the meeting, make the meal, show up for the child, sit through the service, go back home, try again the next morning. Maybe that version was not polished. Maybe they were reactive sometimes. Maybe they were scared in ways they never admitted.
But they kept going.
That counts for something.

We are often harsh with the versions of ourselves that were still learning how to live. We look back and call them foolish, weak, dramatic, naive, angry, or lost. Sometimes we speak about ourselves with a sharpness we would never use on another person who had survived the same season.

But some versions of you were not trying to become impressive. They were trying to stay alive inside a life that felt bigger than their capacity. Some versions of you were making decisions with a nervous system that had been stretched too far. Some versions of you were loving from an empty place, praying with tired words, building with shaky hands, and hoping quietly that one day things would make more sense.
That does not mean every choice was right.

Compassion is not the same as pretending. You can be honest about what hurt others. You can take responsibility for what came from your immaturity, fear, pride, or pain. But responsibility does not require hatred. Growth does not require you to despise the person who had to begin before they knew how.
There is a gentler way to remember yourself.

You can say, I would not do everything the same way now, and still honour the fact that I did not give up then. You can admit that you were messy and still recognise that you were carrying something real. You can grieve what happened without making your younger self the enemy of your healing.
Maybe the version of you that kept going deserves a different kind of sentence.

Not applause. Not excuses. Not a dramatic rewriting of the past. Just mercy. The kind of mercy that says, I understand why you were tired. I understand why you were afraid. I understand why you held on to some things too tightly and let go of others too quickly. I understand that you were doing what you could with what you had.

That kind of mercy can be hard to receive because many of us think self-compassion will make us careless. We think if we soften towards ourselves, we will stop growing. But shame has never been the only teacher. Sometimes people change more deeply when they finally feel safe enough to tell the truth.

God has always known the full story of every version of you. Not only the part people saw. Not only the mistakes that left marks. Not only the chapters you wish you could edit. He saw the context, the pressure, the fear, the small faith, the hidden effort, and the quiet decision to keep breathing when nobody knew how much it took.
So maybe today you can look back without turning away.

Look at the version of you that stayed. The one that made it through the confusing season. The one that did not always choose perfectly, but did not disappear. The one that carried pain without a map and still somehow brought you here.
You do not have to be proud of everything.
But you can be grateful that some version of you kept going long enough for this version to exist.
That is not nothing.
That is a kind of grace.
